title: Massive leg swelling in a 35-year-old male
presentation: A 35-year-old male presents with 3 weeks of progressive bilateral leg swelling now extending to the abdomen, frothy urine, and periorbital edema worse in the mornings. No prior medical history. Vitals: BP 148/96, HR 82, RR 16, Temp 36.9C, SpO2 99%. Exam: massive pitting edema to the thighs, ascites, periorbital puffiness, no rash, no joint swelling.

gold_standard_workup.key_decision_point: Massive proteinuria + hypoalbuminemia + edema + hyperlipidemia = nephrotic syndrome. Urine dipstick showing 3+ protein is key. Renal biopsy determines underlying cause. Minimal change disease is most common in adults — responds to steroids.
